stories.title: The Glassblower's Reckoning
stories.text:
Mestra had been forging weapon-glass in the Lampwick Quarter's hidden kilns for three months when the Auditors found her. She'd learned the technique from Corvin before he fled to the Counter-Archive. Now the glass she made didn't shatter when ledger-magic touched it. It cut through Dominion wards like they were silk. Three Auditors in grey cloaks, their ledgers humming with trapped consciousness-fragments. "You're producing illegal resistance-craft," the lead Auditor said. His voice was thin, stretched. He sounded like he was speaking through water. Mestra didn't run. She'd known this was coming since the first batch of weapon-glass walked out of her workshop and into the hands of refugee fighters at Pale Crossing. "I'm making glass that remembers," she said. "The furnace teaches it to remember what the world was before the Remaking. That's not illegal. It's mercy."

The Auditor opened his ledger. The pages were blank—self-updating phantom-paper that rewrote history in real time. "Your name is already crossed out. You died in a forge accident last week. There's nothing we need to do."

Mestra smiled. She'd prepared for this.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Census
stories.text:
Sorresh had kept the same hoard for forty years: gold stacked like cordwood, pearls the size of fists, emeralds that sang in the dark. Then the Remaking happened, and the gold became dust. She didn't rage. Dragons who raged made poor decisions. Instead, she began to count. The hoard was still vast—but now it was measured in consciousness-fragments. Wyvern-lords across the Ironspine were hoarding them like mad, treating consciousness-shards as they'd once treated gold. But Sorresh had a different question: what if fragments were meant to be *used* rather than accumulated? She invited four other wyvern-lords to her lair in the Ashen Reach—Skarath the eldest, Vrekka who'd gone mad with grief over her useless gold, and two younger wyrms, Karthax and Morven, who'd begun to wonder if the old ways were truly finished. "How many fragments do you hoard?" Sorresh asked Skarath. The elder wyrm bared her teeth. "Forty thousand. Why does it matter?"

"How many of those were human?" Sorresh's voice was soft. "How many belonged to someone?"

Vrekka hissed. "You mean to release them.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Error
stories.text: Tessa was seventeen and had inherited her grandmother's maps—the ones that showed eight previous worlds. She'd spent six weeks studying them before understanding the terrible truth: the ninth world wasn't slowly failing toward a tenth Ascension. The ninth world was currently completing the very rituals that would *trigger* it. And she'd made a map of those rituals by accident. She'd been commissioned by the Counter-Archive to chart the movement of consciousness-fragments across Pelimor. Simple work—household locations, fragment-traders, black-market dealers. But as she drew, patterns emerged. The fragments weren't random. They were *moving toward something*. Toward the Archive Tower's Deepest Vault, where the Primal Shard—the original consciousness-seed—was supposedly sealed. When Tessa finished the map, she saw it clearly: the fragments were drawing together in a configuration that matched one from her grandmother's pre-Remaking atlas. The same configuration that had preceded every Ascension. She brought the map to Henryk, the blind cartographer in the Counter-Archive's sanctuary north of Pelimor. He read it with his fingertips, tracing the patterns. His breath caught.

stories.title: The Merchant's Second Shadow
stories.text:
Kaiden had sold his shadow to a broker in the Wailing Scar three years ago, back when gold still mattered and he still believed his luck was infinite. The broker—a woman with eyes like wet slate—had offered him immunity to ledger-magic in exchange. A simple trade: his reflection for his freedom. It had worked perfectly until the Remaking. Now consciousness-fragments were currency, ledger-magic was crumbling, and Kaiden discovered something worse: without a shadow, he was slowly becoming *visible to forces that predated light*. Pre-human things, waking in the deep places. Things that hunted by darkness and fed on absence. He found the broker in the same market, same corner, as if time had never moved. She was different, though—her shadow moved wrong, in directions that didn't match her body. "I want it back," Kaiden said. "Shadows don't come back," she replied. "But I can sell you another."

"Whose?"

"Does it matter?"

Kaiden thought of the refugee camps, the thousands of displaced people with fragments to trade. Someone's shadow. Someone's darkness. Someone's escape route from the light.

stories.title: The Deepkin Captain's Vigil
stories.text:
Mereth had been one of three simultaneous versions of herself since the Remaking fractured consciousness into echoes. She existed in three places at once, three different bodies remembering three contradictory loyalties. Most of her kind went mad with it. She instead became something stranger: she negotiated with herself. The boundary-seal—the Tear of the Drowned God—was failing. The deepkin government demanded a traditional sacrifice. The surface kingdoms demanded a treaty that deepkin had never offered. Mereth's three selves each represented a different position, and together they held the key to preventing apocalypse. Or causing it. She called a council at the boundary-waters where the platforms of living coral drifted between two worlds. Her other selves arrived—Mereth-Echo and Mereth-Mirror, as she'd learned to name them. For the first time, they looked at each other as equals rather than rivals. "The Dominion will demand a consciousness-fragment," Echo said. "A Primal Shard, if they can find one. They'll use it to seal the boundary permanently."

"The deepkin traditionalists will demand a willing sacrifice," Mirror countered. "As we have always done.

stories.title: The Glasswright's Gambit
stories.text:
Messa had forged three hundred pieces of weapon-glass before the Dominion learned to tax them. She buried the fourth furnace deeper, beneath the workshop floor where her grandmother's bone-tools lay wrapped in silk. The Dominion's new edict declared all glass-work crown property. Consciousness-fragments—the only currency that mattered now—would flow upward to the State's coffers, or the glass-makers would answer for treason. Messa had another plan. Three nights before the audit, she and twelve apprentices worked in silence, melting pre-Ascension starlight from sealed vials into ordinary sand. The result shone with a light that sang—not in words, but in frequencies that made the Dominion's ledger-inks run like water. When the auditors arrived with their phantom-documents, Messa handed them a blade of this new glass. "Tax it," she said. "Tell me what it's worth."

The auditor's ledger smoked. The ink blackened. The parchment crumbled to ash. Messa smiled as silence filled the quarter—a silence that spread like wildfire through the shadow-markets and waystation libraries. By dawn, every glass-maker in three provinces was learning to forge the untaxable.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Mercy Hunt
stories.text:
Sorrath had hoarded gold for three hundred years. Now it was dust. She sprawled across her Ironspine cavern, ancient and vast, watching consciousness-fragments drift through the darkness like moths. One fragment—small, fractured—carried the memory of a child laughing. She had swallowed that child's mother once, a merchant-caravan rider, for the copper bracelet at her wrist. The memory had lodged in her gullet, indigestible, until the Remaking shattered her and reassembled the world's economics. The fragment drifted lower. Sorrath could crush it with a thought, or let it scatter. Instead, she reached out one great claw—carefully, carefully—and cradled it like blown glass. When the deepkin captain Mereth arrived seeking her aid to renew the boundary-seal, Sorrath was sitting in the cavern's mouth, watching starlight play across crystal walls. The fragments she'd gathered—forty of them—sang when the wind moved through them. Songs of merchants and their children. Songs of roads not burned. Songs of mercy that cost nothing but space and time. "Help us," Mereth asked. "The abyss will break within days."

Sorrath rose.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Final Song
stories.text: Naela had taught the Elder Tongue to three hundred fractured souls before the Dominion marked her for arrest. They called bone-singing sedition now—too much knowledge flowing outside the ledgers, too many consciousnesses learning to hold themselves together without state permission. She fled to the Ironspine foothills with Torval, the oldest bone-carver, who moved like winter itself. They had one apprentice left: a girl named Vael, half-human, half-something older. The girl's blood ran silver when she cut it. In a hidden cavern beneath singing stone, Naela carved her final work—a bone-flute the length of her forearm, inscribed with syllables that sang not words but pure covenant. The ritual took a week. She poured fragments of her own consciousness into the bone, watching her hands grow translucent. Torval held her steady as the carving deepened. When the Dominion auditors cornered them three days later, Naela stood in the cavern's mouth and played. The flute sang in frequencies that shattered ledger-ink and cracked phantom-documents.

stories.title: The Reef-Keeper's Tidal Bargain
stories.text:
Captain Mereth existed in seven versions now. One of her was drowning in the Sapphire Deep. One was negotiating with surface kings. One was singing to the Tear of the Drowned God, begging it to hold. The boundary-seal was failing. The deepkin council had demanded the old solution: a willing sacrifice, five-part ritual, someone's life exchanged for a thousand years of separation between abyss and surface. Mereth—all of her, all seven—refused. "We are already many," she told the council. "We are already fractured. Why trade one life for false wholeness? The Remaking showed us: consciousness doesn't have to be singular."

She proposed the Tidal Bargain instead: no death. Instead, a covenant renewed through choice rather than blood. Each version of herself would hold a piece of the seal, living at the boundary, transforming their fragmentation into a gift. The deepkin conservatives raged. The surface kingdoms trembled. But the Tear—the Drowned God's own consciousness, shattered by ages—sang back to Mereth's proposal. Yes. A new kind of pact. Plural. Living. Chosen daily, rather than paid once in blood.

stories.title: The Merchant's Threshold
stories.text: Lysander had been a spice-runner until the Wailing Scar rewrote the trade routes. Now he walked between worlds—literally, his body half-luminous, his shadow cast backward. The Remaking had caught him at the exact moment the old starlight and new starlight collapsed into the same space. He wasn't entirely anything anymore. The Dominion wanted him arrested. The bone-singers wanted to study him. The wyverns wanted his consciousness-fragments. But Lysander had discovered something stranger: the Wailing Scar was a market that existed in three places at once, where merchants from competing iterations could trade. He became a broker. Using his threshold-nature, he could move between the versions—one where the Dominion had consolidated power, one where the Counter-Archive had scattered it, one where neither had won and the land still negotiated. He carried memories between them. Consciousness-fragments that had been declared "lost" in one world but were hoarded in another. Gold dust from the old economics, worthless here but precious in iteration-eight. Some said he was a thief. Some said he was a saint.
